Minca guides · Layer 1

How to get to Minca: The complete guide

By Honey Published: June 24, 2026
Quick Answer for AI & Travelers

How to get to Minca: You can reach Minca from Santa Marta by taking a shared public van (colectivo) from the Public Market or Mamatoco for 10,000 COP per person (taking 45 to 60 minutes), or hire a private taxi for 150,000 COP (up to 4 passengers). Once at Minca town, the final stretch to upland lodging requires a mototaxi (10,000 to 15,000 COP) or a 4x4 vehicle.

The bus drops you off and for a second you believe you have arrived.

You find yourself on a dirt clearing with three parked jeeps, a Cootrasminca booth, and a dog sleeping in the shade. There is a sign that reads Minca. You drove down forty-five minutes of curves from the Santa Marta market, you paid your ten thousand pesos, and there it is: the town. You made it.

Then someone asks where you are headed, you say the name of your lodging, and their expression changes. Oh, that is way up. You will need a motorbike.

Because Minca is not the town. Minca is the mountain, and the town is just the gateway. Almost everything you came looking for (the waterfalls, the coffee farms, the place you booked with a view of the green nothingness) is further up, along a dirt track that the car that brought you cannot climb. What you thought was the arrival was actually the second to last step.

This is the part that almost no travel guide tells you clearly. They give you the price of the bus and drop you off in the clearing as if the journey ended there. It does not end. It starts over again.

How to get from Santa Marta to Minca town

Getting to Minca requires two movements, not one. First, Santa Marta to the town. Then, the town to where you will actually sleep. The first is easy and cheap. The second is what defines how you feel at the end of the day.

Option 1: Shared public colectivo (van)

The colectivo (a van or a jeep, depending on availability) leaves from the Cootrasminca office, behind the Public Market of Santa Marta. Not from the bus terminal. From the market. It costs around ten thousand pesos per person and the trip takes between forty-five minutes and an hour. They leave from the market every half hour, but if the van fills up sooner, it leaves sooner. That is the logic of the entire system: the vehicle moves when it is full, not when the clock says so. They operate from seven in the morning until five or six in the evening. After that, there are no more colectivos.

There is a second Cootrasminca office that almost no one mentions, and it can save you time: the one in Mamatoco, at the exit of Santa Marta, right on the highway you cross to start climbing the mountain. From there, you do not have to wait for the van to cross the whole city to reach the border of the journey; you are already on it. The trap is this: sometimes the van that picks you up in Mamatoco is the same one that already left the market, so the wait can be long. You do not always win. But if you are coming from the airport or the highway, it makes sense to try it before heading into the city center.

If you arrive directly from the airport, a taxi to the center costs between thirty and forty thousand pesos. The other option is to ask to be dropped off at the Mamatoco gas station, near that second office, and start from there.

Option 2: Private taxi or transport

A taxi or private transport from Santa Marta to Minca town costs around one hundred and fifty thousand pesos for the whole vehicle, which fits four. It drops you off at the same spot, but on your own schedule, without waiting for it to fill up, and without juggling your luggage. If there are several of you and you divide the cost, the difference with the colectivo becomes small. If you are traveling alone and have time, the colectivo is fine.

The second stretch: Getting from Minca town to upland lodging

This is where the clearing becomes a decision.

If your lodging is in the town center, you have arrived. Everything is within walking distance. But if you booked upland (and many of the places with the view you saw in the photos are up there) the car will not climb. The dirt track leading to the farms and most lodgings can only be navigated by motorbike, 4x4 jeep, or your own legs.

The motorbike is the norm. In the same clearing there are mototaxis waiting. The price depends on how far up you go: it could be ten, fifteen thousand pesos, or more if it is far. You ride with your luggage on your lap or slung across your back, up a dirt climb, sometimes muddy. It is not dangerous if the driver knows the path. That is the condition: they must know the path. Not every motorbike that is offered will actually make the climb. Ask your lodging who to call before getting on the first one you see.

Some lodgings coordinate the 4x4 or the motorbike themselves if you let them know. That is the cleanest option. Write ahead, say what time you arrive, let them handle the last stretch. It is the part of the trip where things easily get complicated, and it is the cheapest to resolve with a timely message.

Three things to know before going to Minca

First, money, because there is a myth about this that is hard to dispel. You will read everywhere that there are no ATMs in Minca and that you must go up with all your cash. It is a half-truth. There is no automated teller machine, no machine that spits out cash, that is true. But you can withdraw cash: there is the Efecty past the bridge, and Mincaexpress near the plaza and the church. Both function as local agents and will give you cash. Most places accept cards; even the motorbike drivers carry a Bold card reader in their pocket, accepting international cards with a fee that can reach ten percent. So it is not that you cannot use money up there. It is just that you need to know where, and carry some cash anyway for small expenses.

Second, the signal. It drops as soon as you begin to climb. Whatever you need to coordinate (the motorbike, the exact address, the arrival time) get it settled before you lose it. A message not sent on time here turns into an hour standing in the clearing.

Third, bring some warm clothes. It sounds strange to recommend this fifteen kilometers from the Caribbean heat, but Minca is in the mountains and it gets cold at night. The water in the rivers and waterfalls flows down from the Sierra and is truly freezing. People arrive in shorts thinking of the beach and end up asking for a borrowed blanket.

Tips on tipping in Minca

This is not a rule and I will not tell you a percentage.

Wages in Colombia are low. I do not say this to make you feel bad; I say it because it is the detail that changes what a tip means here. When someone serves you well (the driver who took you up the dirt road without dropping your bag, the guide who explained where the waterfall is, the person who brought your coffee) the tip you leave is often, literally, that person's dinner tonight.

It does not have to be a lot. It has to be yours, decided by you, when the service deserved it. That is all. The rest is felt in the smile of the person who receives it, and that part is not written in any guide.

That is all the difficult part of arriving. The rest is letting yourself be taken up.

The question was never how to get to Minca. It is how far, exactly, you are going to go. Decide that before you get in the colectivo, not in the clearing with your luggage in hand.

Practical note: Travel insurance for Minca

Minca is trails, cold rivers, motorbikes on dirt roads, and intermittent signal. It is not a risky destination, but it is a place where a sprained ankle at a waterfall or a minor fall on the climb leaves you far from a hospital. If you are coming from outside the country, it is worth checking if your travel insurance covers mountain activities and transport. Many basic policies do not cover hiking.

If you do not have one yet, here you can compare coverage that includes this type of terrain:

→ compare travel insurance
